content: When you first encounter closures in Rust, the most confusing part is that a closure can occupy zero bytes of memory. This happens when a closure does not capture any variables from its environment and does not contain free functions or generic type parameters; such a non-capturing closure is effectively just a pointer to a code block with no associated heap data, making it a zero-sized type or ZST in the eyes of the compiler. Think of this as a mental model where you visualize a function call not as executing a large subroutine stored on the stack or heap, but simply as jumping to an address and running instructions immediately; because there is nothing to carry along with the closure value itself, it collapses into a single register operation. The compiler takes advantage of this by devirtualizing these calls, meaning it can replace what would normally be a function pointer indirection with direct inline code generation for that specific call site, effectively eliminating the virtual dispatch overhead entirely. This optimization becomes particularly relevant when designing generic callbacks versus trait object callbacks because trait objects introduce heap allocation and vtable lookup overhead every time you invoke them, whereas monomorphizing non-capturing closures allows the compiler to generate specialized machine code directly for each instantiation of your generic type without needing a runtime abstraction layer. When you write a closure like fn f() { } without capturing any variables or referencing other types, the resulting object is so small that it fits entirely within a register and can be inlined by the optimizer during compilation. This insight shifts your mental model from thinking about closures as just convenient syntax sugar to recognizing them as potential performance primitives when used correctly in high-frequency call paths like event loops or tight numerical kernels where every cycle counts. The trade-off is that while this optimization gives you raw speed and predictability, it sacrifices the flexibility of polymorphism unless you are careful with type constraints, so you must decide upfront whether your code prioritizes runtime adaptability or compile-time performance. By understanding that a non-capturing closure is merely an addressable block of instructions without state, you can write more efficient Rust code by leveraging devirtualization to eliminate unnecessary indirection and avoid the hidden costs of heap allocation inherent in trait object implementations.
